steps.title: A door that opens with a code

narration.default: Hotel rooms, office entrances, even some house front doors — they don't use a metal key any more. Tap the right number on a keypad and the lock clicks open. Today you'll build a tiny version: a 4x4 keypad reads the keys you press, and a servo motor swings the lock open or closed. To keep things simple, pressing 1 unlocks the door and pressing the star key locks it again. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace.

A 4x4 keypad has 16 keys arranged as 4 rows and 4 columns. The Arduino scans the rows and columns to figure out which key you pressed — clever, but it needs to know which pins go where. Open the Keypad category and drag a setup keypad block into setup. Leave the type on 4x4. The row pins are 2, 3, 4, 5 and the column pins are 6, 7, 8, 9 — type those in to match the wiring.

steps.title: Wake up the servo on pin 10

narration.default: Open the Servo category and drag the servo.attach block below the keypad setup. Set its pin to 10. This tells the Arduino which pin the servo is wired to — without it, every later servo command would error out because the Arduino wouldn't know where the motor lives.

steps.title: A box for the pressed key

narration.default: Open Variables and click Make a Variable. Name it key. This will hold whichever key the user just pressed on the keypad.

Drag a set key block into the loop. From the Keypad category, drag the key pressed on keypad reporter and drop it in place of the 0. Now every loop the program asks the keypad: 'which key is being pressed right now?' and stashes the answer in our key variable.

steps.title: Decision time: did they press unlock?

narration.default: Open Control and drag an if block below the set key block. We'll use it to decide what the lock does when the user presses the unlock key.

Open Operators and drag an equals block into the if condition slot. Drop the key variable on the left side. Type 1 on the right side. The condition reads: if the user just pressed 1 — unlock the door.
Open the Servo category and drag a servo write block into the if branch. Set pin to 10 and angle to 90. Pressing 1 swings the servo to the 90-degree mark — the bolt slides back and the door is open.

steps.title: Decision two: did they press lock?

narration.default: Drag a second if block below the first one. This branch handles locking the door when the user presses the star key.
Drag another equals block into the second if's condition. Put the key variable on the left and type a star character on the right. When the user taps star, the lock should snap shut.
Drag another servo write block into the second if branch. Keep pin on 10 but set angle to 0 — the servo swings back, the bolt slides into place, and the door is locked again.
Open the Simulator and click the plus button. Choose Membrane Keypad and click Save. The keypad is wired to rows 2-5 and columns 6-9 just like our code expects.
Click the plus again and add a Servo Motor on pin 10. The lock motor is now connected.
Click Start. Your program compiles and the keypad starts listening for key presses.

Press 1 on the keypad — the servo swings to 90 and the door is unlocked. Press the star key — the servo returns to 0 and the door is locked again. Try this: change 1 to a different unlock key for your own secret combination. Or for a real challenge, only unlock when the user types a multi-digit code in the right order — that's how real keypad locks work.
